steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The team’s claim has two parts: (1) a genetic mutation strongly affects dog size, and (2) that mutation existed long before humans began deliberately breeding dogs (i.e., before 12,000 years ago). A supporting finding must address the timing of the mutation — showing it predates human breeding.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘mutation exists in an ancestor dating to 53,000 years ago’: 53,000 years ago is well before the 12,000-year threshold for human breeding, directly confirming the mutation predated selective breeding. ✓
  • Choice B — ‘wolves are related to dogs but are much bigger than Pomeranians’: a size comparison between wolves and Pomeranians says nothing about when the mutation arose. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘some breeds without the mutation are very large’: this describes the mutation’s absence in large breeds, but doesn’t address the timing claim. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘dogs vary in size more than any other land mammal’: overall size variation across species doesn’t speak to when this specific mutation appeared. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
